title: File-backed preferences are not durable in typical Next production deployments

evidence.quote:
function saveAll(data: Record<string, NotificationPrefs>) {
  fs.writeFileSync(PREFS_FILE, JSON.stringify(data, null, 2), "utf-8");

## Deploy on Vercel

The easiest way to deploy your Next.js app is to use the [Vercel Platform](https://vercel.com/new?utm_medium=default-template&filter=next.js&utm_source=create-next-app&utm_campaign=create-next-app-readme) from the creators of Next.js.

reasoning: The API stores user preferences by writing notifications.json under process.cwd(). In common Next production environments such as serverless or horizontally scaled deployments, local filesystem writes are ephemeral, instance-local, or disallowed, so preferences can disappear after redeploys/restarts or diverge across instances. The README's deployment guidance points users toward this class of deployment.
recommendation: Move notification preferences to durable shared storage such as a database, KV store, or another persistence service configured for the deployment environment. If local filesystem storage is only for development, gate it clearly and fail closed in production.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No linked tests are included, and the provided project context does not show deployment-mode tests or persistence tests across process restarts/instances.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add an integration test around the selected persistent store, or at least a production configuration test that fails if the route is configured to use process-local file storage.
minimumFixScope: Replace process.cwd() JSON-file persistence for this route with a production-safe storage adapter or explicitly disable the route outside local development.
